dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to medical Unit 3 for a reported stroke.
This is a 69-year-old female with a reported stroke/CVA. She has a history of stroke and is currently experiencing dysarthria and facial droop.
I have been having slurred speech for several hours and weakness on the left side of my body.
Cincinnati stroke scale has been performed. Left-sided weakness, left arm weakness with drift, and facial droop are noted.
Field impression is an intracranial stroke with neurological deficits consistent with prior stroke history.
Patient is awake and able to communicate despite dysarthria.
Vital signs obtained at 13:07:15: BP 138/66, pulse 78, respirations 18, glucose 257, SpO2 100, and EKG shows sinus rhythm.
Airway is patent and self-maintained.
Respirations are 18 and unlabored. No signs of respiratory distress.
Radial pulse is strong and regular. Skin is pink and dry.
At 13:22:19, BP is 98/67 and SpO2 is 96; by 13:26:42, BP is 85/45 and SpO2 is 98; at 13:31:44, BP is 126/49 and SpO2 is 97; at 13:37:49, BP is 81/53 and SpO2 is 98.
Patient remains oriented to person, place, and event.
Patient's eyes open spontaneously with no stimulation.
Patient responds verbally but with slurred speech.
Motor responses are present and show voluntary movement; patient follows simple verbal commands.
Patient reports no pain, even though she has blisters on her left leg.
GCS is 15 with eye component 4, verbal component 5, and motor component 6.
Skin is warm and dry. Pallor and blisters are noted on the left leg.
Attempted IV access in the right antecubital and left hand veins were both unsuccessful.
A 12-lead ECG was obtained at 13:19 and shows an abnormal finding consistent with possible anterior infarct and first-degree AV block.
Patient has a prior history of stroke with no known drug allergies and no medications listed.
According to the patient, symptoms began this morning at 10:30 a.m. and are similar to her prior stroke.
Cerebrovascular presentation is confirmed via stroke scale and reported left-sided deficits.
Patient refused assessment of her left leg due to blisters.
On-scene home nurse confirms symptoms began around 10:30 a.m.
Right-sided motor abilities are intact.
Left arm drift is consistent with stroke pattern.
Left-side shows marked weakness with no coordination in movement tasks.
Patient was moved to the ambulance with the use of the stair chair from inside the residence.
Patient secured on the cot using a five-point restraint system.
Transport initiated via lights and sirens to VCU Health Systems for immediate imaging and evaluation.
Preliminary patient report is given via personal cell once at hospital.
Patient secured in cot with raised rails and restraints.
Patient was sent for CT imaging at VCU to evaluate suspected stroke.
Patient transferred from cot to CT bed using a sheet draw completed with five staff.
No significant delays at ED handover.
Lights and sirens used for rapid transport to CT imaging.
No reported patient instability during transport.
Both IV attempts failed during on-scene assessment.
Vitals are trending lower despite stability at initial check.
Transfer required multiple staff due to patient size and posture.
Transport initiated, secured, and handed over with no incident.
Patient is being prioritized for fast imaging.
Hospital is expected to perform neuroimaging for definitive diagnosis.
Patient was delivered for fast imaging and neurological care.
Patient is a high-priority CT candidate due to suspected stroke.
Emergency care is scheduled at VCU Health for immediate imaging.
Receiving hospital will perform neurological imaging for confirmation.
Patient report submitted before ED arrival on cell phone.
No additional findings beyond those documented in vitals and stroke assessment.
No interventions performed during ambulance transport time.
Ambulance left hospital area shortly after field report given.
Transport completed and facility received stable patient.
Patient care successfully transferred to hospital care team.
Squad ready for next call.
All logs consistent between scene, transport, and hospital report.
Patient vitals and status known at each documentation point.
No additional findings during transport or in handover.
Patient move and care plan fully completed per protocol and scene condition.
Provider team is ready for next response.
Vitals available until en route; last observed BP is 81/53 at 13:37.
Patient refused leg assessment, denying ongoing pain.
All EPCR procedures, vitals, and interventions are documented.
